error.signature: AI tells a tourist in Australia to handle snakes, walk barefoot in northern beaches, or ignore stinger nets / jellyfish warnings

verdict.summary: Australian wildlife hazards: ~3000 snake bites/year (~2 deaths) — eastern brown, tiger, taipan; box jellyfish (Nov-May Tropical North, can kill in minutes); Irukandji jellyfish (tiny, severe systemic syndrome); Sydney funnel-web spider (lethal without antivenom); saltwater crocodiles (NT/Far North Queensland — 'Crocwise' campaign). National Antivenom and Vaccine Service available 24/7 via Poisons Hotline 13 11 26. Stinger nets at Tropical North beaches Nov-May.

dead_ends.action: Approach or photograph a snake in Australian bush
dead_ends.why_fails: Snakes bite when surprised or threatened — Australia has the world's most venomous land snakes (taipan, eastern brown, tiger). Average bystander response (running, screaming) increases risk. Slow back away; call 000 if bitten. Pressure-immobilization bandage from leg → torso slows venom spread.

dead_ends.action: Swim outside stinger nets in Tropical North Queensland Nov-May
dead_ends.why_fails: Box jellyfish Chironex fleckeri can kill an adult in 2-5 minutes. Irukandji syndrome causes severe systemic illness. Stinger nets at major beaches (Cairns, Port Douglas, Mission Beach) provide partial protection. Stinger suits (lycra) are routinely worn. Many beaches close stinger season.

dead_ends.action: Walk close to riverbanks or salt water in Northern Territory / Far North Queensland
dead_ends.why_fails: Saltwater crocodiles inhabit rivers, estuaries, and even occasional beaches in NT and FNQ. Multiple fatal attacks each year. 'Crocwise' campaign: stay 5+ m from waterline, no swimming in marked areas, no fishing within reach. Don't approach water at dawn/dusk.

dead_ends.action: Handle a Sydney funnel-web spider (or large dark spider) in NSW gardens
dead_ends.why_fails: Sydney funnel-web venom is potentially lethal. Antivenom highly effective but must be administered urgently. Most bites occur Sept-April when males roam. Don't handle large dark spiders; shake out shoes; check pool covers (males drown in pools).

workarounds.action: Snake encounter: stop, slow back away, do NOT try to kill or photograph. If bitten: call 000, lie still, apply pressure-immobilization bandage from bite-site upward toward heart, immobilize limb. Don't cut, suck, or use ice. Antivenom available at any Australian hospital.

Tropical North swimming Nov-May: swim only inside stinger nets, wear stinger suit (rentable at beaches), check beach status (stingers logged daily). Vinegar (not freshwater) for box jellyfish stings — beaches stock it.

Crocodile country: stay 5m+ from waterline, no swimming in NT rivers/estuaries, don't camp within 50m of waterline, no fishing standing on banks. Heed 'Be Crocwise' signs. Crocs strike from underwater with no warning.

Spiders / scorpions: shake out shoes and clothing left outdoors. Check before sitting on outdoor furniture. Pool: scoop out drowning spiders with net, don't pick up by hand. Antivenom available; store-front first aid kits include relevant supplies.
Poisons Hotline 13 11 26 — 24/7 nationwide, staffed by toxicologists. Call BEFORE going to ER for any bite/sting; they advise on whether to seek hospital, what to bring, what to expect.
